content:
Think Node.js is Single Threaded ?? Nahh man.. Here’s what happens Under the Hood

7 min read
3 hours ago

--

Before going deep into the Async nature of JS ..can u predict the output of the code below:
console.log("1");
setTimeout(() => {
console.log("2");
}, 0);
console.log("3");
setTimeout(() => {
console.log("4");
}, 0);
console.log("5");Take your time and try to debug that code …
Ready with your ANSWER ???
Here is the output -
1
3
5
2
4
Now let’s Debug this step by step and burst the myth that Node.js is single threaded ..
JavaScript executes your code from top to bottom , So for now the Call Stack is empty
when JS reaches here ->
that console.log("1"); enters the Call Stack , then as soon as it gets executed it will get popped out of the Call Stack
Press enter or click to view image in full size

and the Call stack becomes empty again
Now it reaches the setTimeout timer function
Now the setTimeout timer function enters the Call Stack
From here things become interesting
the setTimeout does not run on the JavaScript Call Stack
what happen here is that Node handles this timer to it’s Timer system which is implemented by the Libuv and the OS
Doesn’t Understand anything ??
Don’t Worry Let’s make it Simple
Before Going deep into it Ask yourself Why JS is doing this ???
the answer is -> Node moves slow I/O operations outside the JavaScript main Thread so the single JavaScript remains free to process other requests
That’s it !!!
okay now let’s debug the problem step by step
Initial State->
Call Stack [ ]
Timer System [ ]
Callback Queue[ ]
Now let’s move to Line 1 :
console.log("1");
Now console.log enters the Call Stack , get’s executed and prints the output 1 on the console
then ???
Now Call Stack becomes Empty again
Line 2 :
setTimeout(() => {
console.log("2");
}, 0);
so now the timer enters the call Stack , Now comes the twist , Java Script only knows how to execute math , Variables synchronous code etc.. it has next to no built in idea of system clock , hard drives or network cards …
Then who register this timer if JS can’t ??
Here comes the man !!!!
“Libuv” does that
Libuv is a high performance C library wrapped inside Node js .. It basically gives Node.js access to the Underlying Operating System
Back to the Flow —
So now what happens is when the setTimeout enters the stack the Node.js immediately hands the timer to libuv ..
where libuv registers the timer in Min-Heap
So the timer will be in the Libuv Timer system as long as the Timer expires and the currently executing script finishes
when the timer finishes the callback function in the timer will move to the Callback Queue and waits there until the synchronous code get’s Executed
So for now the Call Stack is empty because the node hands the timer to libuv
Line 3 :
console.log("3");
Same pattern like first one -> console.log("3") enters the Call Stack and get’s executed and then popped out of the Call Stack
Line 4 :
setTimeout(() => {
console.log("4");
}, 0);
Another Timer , The whole process for this will remain same for what we discuss for the previous timer ..
So for now -> Timer system will look like this ->
Keep in mind -> The CallStack is still Empty
Line 5 :
console.log("5");
The Call Stack is empty so now Console.log("5") enters the Call Stack -> get’s printed and then popped out
So now, the Main Script Ends —
until now only the Synchronous code finished executing
So currently the Call Stack is Empty
Now the Event Loop start checking the Queues and both the timer will be expired till now
Now let’s have a look at the Callback queue -> (There will be a Catch in Queue’s ahead so be ready for that)
so we have both the callback there now the Event Loop starts Processing them one by one
Now the Callback of 2 get’s Processed
So , now the Call stack is currently empty so the callback will now enter the Call Stack -> get’s executed ->then popped out -> Call Stack becomes empty again
Now the process repeats for the Callback of 4
and after all this we get out final output ->
1
3
5
2
4
Just keep this Entire flow in your mind ->
ufffff .. Exhausted ?? Even I am , while Writing this …
Now let’s just have a quick recap of what we Understands via Real Life Example ->
Let’s Imagine a Chef(The Call Stack) at a Party Serving all the guest by itself (Single Threaded Java Script) , Now he receives three orders
1. VIP guest who are standing at the Counter (Synchronous Code) , the chef serves them first no matter what
2. Now the Needed ones (Microtask Queue->Promises) , the time chef free’s with the VIP guest chef will serve them
3. Distant Delivery Orders (Macrotask Queue ->Set Timeout) — When the Chef is done with serving the upper 2 chef will serve them
Now u will ask What is Microtask and Macrotask Queue ?
Understand it like this
There are 2 major types of queues :
1. Macrotask Queue , which have setTimeout , setInterval , I/O callbacks , HTTP requests etc
2.Microtask Queue , which have Promise.then() , catch() , finally() etc
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Now the most Intresting part ->
The Event Loop always processes:
Call Stack
↓
ALL Microtasks
↓
ONE Macrotask
↓
repeat
means Microtask have a sort of priority over the Macrotasks
Feel’s overwhelming ??
Just try to guess the output for this one -
console.log("A");
setTimeout(() => {
console.log("B");
}, 0);
Promise.resolve().then(() => {
console.log("C");
});
Promise.resolve().then(() => {
console.log("D");
});
console.log("E");Just remember one Golden Rule ->
The Event Loop will never touch a macrotask if there is even a single microtask left in the queue.
Ohk Let’s solve this one ->
The Synchronous code will Execute First , so console.log("A") will enter the Call Stack -> gets executed -> popped out of the Stack -> Call Stack became Empty
Now for the setTimeout , Node hands it to the Libuv Timer System and when the timer Expired in which Queue does it go ???
YES !!Exactly , it will go into the Macro Task Queue and the Promise one’s will go to the Microtask Queue
Now as the Call Stack is Empty , console.log("E"); get’s into the Call Stack->Get’s Executed -> popped out ->Call Stack becomes Empty again ..
Till now the Output is -
A
E
as they are the synchronous code
Now the Eventloop Starts Processing the Callbacks and Promises from the Queues ..
Till now this is how the Queue’s looks like
what do you think which will get’s into the Call Stack first ??

Remember the Golden Rule ,
YES , The Microtask queue will get executed first so this
Promise.resolve().then(() => {
console.log("C");
});
will get into the Call Stack , till now the Promise was already Fullfiled so , we will get C as the output and then it will get popped out of the Call Stack -> Call Stack become Empty again ..
Now
Promise.resolve().then(() => {
console.log("D");
});
this will get into the Call Stack -> get’s Executed -> popped out -> Call Stack becomes Empty again
Till now the Output is
A
E
C
D
Now the Event Loop Checks that the MicroTasks queue is now empty , so now it will move to the Macrotasks Queue
Now at last by the grace of god, the Callback ->
{
console.log("B");
}
will get into the Call Stack -> get’s executed -> popped out -> the Call Stack becomes Empty again
so the final output will be ->
A
E
C
D
B
Congratulations It’s doneeee !!!!!!!
But .. But .. But ..
Everything comes with Flaws…
There is a way by which u can literally freeze a Node.js sever
how ??
If a Microtask generates another Microtask, the Event Loop will stay right there and execute that new one too.. what happen there is the Event Loop will never move on to process user requests or UI rendering ..
Cool right ???
Let me know any weird bug of Callback that you guys spends hours on 🥸
That’s all for this Article Guysss ,
Happy Coding !!!
